title: Capstone: Assemble a Retrieval Pipeline
concept: Combining hybrid search, query rewriting, and multi-query into one retrieval pipeline; 'Next up: Reranking Deep Dive.'.

exercises.prompt: When benchmarking different retrieval strategies, what does the 'recall' metric measure?

options.text: The fraction of relevant documents successfully retrieved from the database.
The speed at which the database returns search results.
The total storage size of the database index.

exercises.explain: Recall is the percentage of total relevant documents that your retrieval system successfully finds and returns. Benchmarking helps us find the strategy with the highest recall.

cards.title: Powering Up Your Search
cards.body: We have tested individual search tools, but in production, real-world questions are messy and complex. By linking query rewriting, multi-query expansion, and hybrid search into a single pipeline, we can capture the absolute best context for our LLM.

cards.title: What is a Retrieval Pipeline?
cards.body: A retrieval pipeline chains multiple search techniques together. Instead of relying on a single query or a single search index, it cleans the query, expands it into multiple viewpoints, searches both keyword and semantic databases, and merges the results for a far more robust retrieval.

cards.title: The Newsroom Pipeline
cards.body: Think of a retrieval pipeline like a professional newsroom. Query rewriting is the editor refining a messy headline, multi-query is sending out three reporters to find different angles, and hybrid search is checking both digital databases and physical paper archives to get the full story.

cards.title: A Pipeline in Action
cards.body: When a user asks 'How do I fix engine knock?', the pipeline rewrites it to 'troubleshoot engine metallic pinging', triggers three alternative searches, queries both a vector database and an SQL keyword index, and pools the best 10 documents.

cards.title: The 'One-Size-Fits-All' Myth
cards.body: Many beginners think using a single, perfect vector database is enough. In practice, combining different strategies in a pipeline consistently outperforms any single strategy, catching relevant documents that a single vector search would miss.

cards.title: Single vs. Pipeline Retrieval
cards.body: Comparing a single-point lookup to an integrated retrieval pipeline.

left.text: One query goes to one vector database. Misses exact keywords or alternative phrasings.

right.text: Query is rewritten, expanded, and sent to both keyword & vector databases. Maximum coverage.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes the correct order of operations in a standard retrieval pipeline?

options.text: Vector search first, then query rewriting, then LLM generation, then keyword search.
Preprocess the query (rewrite/expand), run hybrid search, and then collect the retrieved documents.
Send the query directly to the LLM, then use the LLM's response to run a keyword search.
Run keyword search only, and discard vector embeddings entirely to save time.

exercises.explain: Query rewriting and multi-query expansion are preprocessing steps that fix typos and expand search phrasing before the actual search indexes are queried.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Assembling multiple retrieval strategies into a single pipeline generally yields higher recall than relying on a single vector search.
exercises.explain: Combining multiple strategies like rewriting and hybrid search catches a wider variety of relevant documents, resulting in higher overall recall.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of a pipeline component:
exercises.sentence: A pipeline uses hybrid search to query both dense vector embeddings and sparse keyword indexes.
exercises.explain: Hybrid search allows the pipeline to query both vector indexes (for meaning) and keyword indexes (for exact terms like serial numbers or names), combining the strengths of both.

exercises.prompt: Identify the pipeline setup that represents an automated retrieval engineering pipeline:

options.text: A human developer manually copy-pastes queries into three different search engines and merges the links in Google Sheets.
An LLM answers a query directly from its pre-trained weights without searching any external databases.
A Python script takes a user query, rewrites it using an LLM, searches vector and BM25 indexes concurrently, and merges the deduplicated results.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an automated, programmatically engineered retrieval pipeline, whereas Options A and B are manual or non-automated processes.

exercises.prompt: You are building a production-grade retrieval pipeline. To maximize retrieval quality for complex questions, which sequence of steps is the most effective workflow?

options.text: Query Rewriting -> Keyword Search only -> Direct LLM Generation
Multi-Query & Rewriting -> Hybrid Search (Vector + Keyword) -> Combined Retrieval Set
Vector Search only -> Multi-Query expansion -> Random filtering
LLM Generation -> Vector DB Storage -> Query Rewriting afterwards

exercises.explain: An optimized pipeline orchestrates multiple steps: it first expands a single query into diverse variations (multi-query), searches both semantic and keyword databases (hybrid search), and combines all the results into a unified set before downstream processing. 'Next up: Reranking Deep Dive.'
